title: Muizz Aman Khan
text:
Hi, I’m Muizz, a Data science / CS student at Lawrence University in Appleton, Wisconsin. My interest in Data science stems from my computer science interests and I wish to improve and utilize those skills to solve real world problems with data.
This site collects my projects and the things I’m working on. Take a look around.

title: Muizz Aman Khan
text:
Skills
Java (FX/FXML), Python, C++, SQL, JavaScript, HTML/CSS, R, TypeScript

title: Muizz Aman Khan
text:
Beyond Class
I love playing video games (especially Pokemon as you’ll see with my projects) and watching anime. I’ve started playing Persona which is a JRPG similar to Pokemon and it has piqued my interest quite a bit. My favorite anime is One Piece (contact me if you want to talk about this stuff, I’m open to it).
I feel like making projects related to those interests allows me to explore the field of data science in my own fun way and gain experience with working on data that people usually don’t work on.

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text: Team project with Martin for ADC 405. Full code and data on GitHub.

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text:
Overview
Using SQL (run through a local duckdb database in R), we built a relational database from three competitive-Pokémon datasets — pokemon, moves, and pokemon_info — covering every Pokémon up to Generation 8, then queried it to answer questions about competitive tiers, typings, base stats, and move power.
One result we liked: even though Normal has the single highest-powered move in the game, Fire moves have the highest median power — Fire is consistently strong, while Normal’s average is dragged down by many weak moves.
Code on this page is collapsed by default — click ► Code on any block to see the SQL or R behind a result. AI tools were used as a coding aid during development and are documented under Sources & tools.

title: SQL Project
text:
[Question 1 — phrased as a real question]
[1 line on what you’re asking → the SQL chunk → the result, then 1 line on what it shows]

title: SQL Project
text:

[2–3 sentences: what you found, and what you’d do next.]

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text:
Database setup
Rather than letting duckdb infer the schema automatically, we define each table explicitly — typing every field, setting defaults, and assigning a primary key — then load the CSVs with COPY. This makes the structure of the data deliberate and reproducible.
The full construction is in the dropdown below.






NoteShow database setup code (schemas + loading)






library(tidyverse)
library(sqldf)     # run SQL against R data frames (used in the plots)
library(duckdb)    # build a duckdb database locally

con_pokemon <- DBI::dbConnect(duckdb::duckdb(), dbdir = "pokemon_db")


USE pokemon_db;


DROP TABLE IF EXISTS pokemon;


CREATE TABLE pokemon (
  name       VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT ' ',  -- competitive name; unique, so primary key
  hp         SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- base stats top out ~255, SMALLINT is plenty
  atk        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  def        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spa        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spd        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spe        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  weight     DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,           -- kg, has decimals -> DOUBLE
  height     DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,           -- m, has decimals -> DOUBLE
  formats    VARCHAR(255),                        -- competitive tier (OU, UU, NFE, LC...)
  generation VARCHAR(255),                        -- game generation code (RB, etc.)
  PRIMARY KEY (name)
);


COPY pokemon FROM 'pokemon_df.csv' (FORMAT CSV, HEADER, NULL 'NA');


DROP TABLE IF EXISTS moves;


CREATE TABLE moves (
  name        VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT ' ',  -- move name; unique, so primary key
  category    VARCHAR(255),                        -- Physical / Special / Non-Damaging
  power       SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- base power; 0 for status moves
  accuracy    SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- 0-100
  priority    SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- can be negative; SMALLINT is signed
  pp          SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- power points
  description VARCHAR(255),                         -- free-text effect
  type        VARCHAR(255),                         -- elemental type
  PRIMARY KEY (name)
);


COPY moves FROM 'moves_df.csv' (FORMAT CSV, HEADER, NULL 'NA');


DROP TABLE IF EXISTS pokemon_info;


CREATE TABLE pokemon_info (
  id              INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,        -- unique row id, so primary key
  pokemon         VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT ' ', -- name; links to pokemon.name
  species_id      INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  height          DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  weight          DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  base_experience SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  type_1          VARCHAR(255),                      -- primary type
  type_2          VARCHAR(255) DEFAULT NULL,         -- single-type pokemon have none -> NULL
  hp              SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  attack          SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  defense         SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  special_attack  SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  special_defense SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  speed           SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  color_1         VARCHAR(255),                      -- hex colour
  color_2         VARCHAR(255) DEFAULT NULL,         -- optional, so nullable
  color_f         VARCHAR(255) DEFAULT NULL,
  egg_group_1     VARCHAR(255),
  egg_group_2     VARCHAR(255) DEFAULT NULL,         -- many have a single egg group -> NULL
  url_icon        VARCHAR(255),
  generation_id   SMALLINT DEFAULT NULL,             -- 1-8; regional forms are NULL
  url_image       VARCHAR(255),
  PRIMARY KEY (id)
);


COPY pokemon_info FROM 'pokemon_info_df.csv' (FORMAT CSV, HEADER, NULL 'NA');

A quick check that all three tables built and loaded correctly:

text:
pokemon

-- To make sure no other outdated tables exist in the database, we drop then create the tables
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS pokemon;


CREATE TABLE pokemon (
  name       VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT ' ',  -- competitive name; unique, so primary key
  hp         SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,         -- base stats top out ~255, SMALLINT 
  atk        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  def        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spa        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spd        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  spe        SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  weight     DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,           -- kg, has decimals -> DOUBLE
  height     DOUBLE NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,           -- m, has decimals -> DOUBLE
  formats    VARCHAR(255),                        -- competitive tier (OU, UU, NFE, LC...)
  generation VARCHAR(255),                        -- game generation code (RB, etc.)
  PRIMARY KEY (name)

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text:
Querying the database
With the database in place, we can answer questions that would be tedious to look up by hand — filtering Pokémon by tier, typing, stats, and more. Each query below runs directly against the database.

The heaviest Pokémon in the OU tier


Code
-- heaviest pokemon in the OU (overused) competitive format (Muizz)
SELECT name, weight, formats
FROM pokemon
WHERE formats = 'OU'
ORDER BY weight DESC
LIMIT 1;



1 records


name
weight
formats




Melmetal
800
OU





The single heaviest Pokémon among those competing in the top “OverUsed” tier.


Strongest Pokémon in OU, by total base stats


Code
-- base stat total of pokemon in OU tier, highest to lowest (Martin)
SELECT pokemon, base_experience, formats, (pokemon_info.hp + pokemon_info.attack + pokemon_info.defense + pokemon_info.special_attack + pokemon_info.special_defense + pokemon_info.speed) AS total
FROM pokemon_info
JOIN pokemon ON pokemon.name = pokemon_info.pokemon
WHERE formats = 'OU'
ORDER BY total DESC
LIMIT 10;



Displaying records 1 - 10


pokemon
base_experience
formats
total




Kyurem
297
OU
660


Zeraora
270
OU
600


Dragonite
270
OU
600


Heatran
270
OU
600


Tyranitar
270
OU
600


Garchomp
270
OU
600


Hydreigon
270
OU
600


Dragapult
300
OU
600


Melmetal
270
OU
600


Zapdos
261
OU
580





Joins the two tables and sums the six base stats on the fly to rank the most statistically powerful Pokémon in the OU tier.


The tallest Pokémon in each generation


Code
-- tallest pokemon in each generation (Muizz)
-- Uses a correlated subquery: for each row, compare its height to the MAX height
-- within that same generation, keeping only the matches.
SELECT generation_id, pokemon, height
FROM pokemon_info AS p1
WHERE height = (
        SELECT MAX(height)
        FROM pokemon_info AS p2
        WHERE p1.generation_id = p2.generation_id
      )
  AND generation_id IS NOT NULL;   -- regional forms have no listed generation (real NULLs)



8 records


generation_id
pokemon
height




1
Onix
8.8


2
Steelix
9.2


3
Wailord
14.5


4
Dialga
5.4


5
Serperior
3.3


6
Yveltal
5.8


7
Celesteela
9.2


8
Eternatus
20.0





A correlated subquery returns both the Pokémon name and its generation in one result — something a simple GROUP BY MAX(height) can’t do on its own.


Poison-type Pokémon in the UU tier


Code
-- poison types in the UU (underused) format (Muizz)
SELECT pokemon, type_1, type_2, pokemon.formats
FROM pokemon_info
JOIN pokemon ON pokemon_info.pokemon = pokemon.name
WHERE (type_1 = 'poison' OR type_2 = 'poison')
  AND pokemon.formats = 'UU';



4 records


pokemon
type_1
type_2
formats




Nidoqueen
poison
ground
UU


Tentacruel
water
poison
UU


Amoonguss
grass
poison
UU


Nihilego
rock
poison
UU





Checks both type slots so dual-typed Poison Pokémon aren’t missed.


Monster egg-group Pokémon, by generation (NFE / LC tiers)


Code
-- count of NFE (not fully evolved) or LC (little cup) Monster egg-group pokemon
-- per generation (Muizz)
SELECT generation_id AS gen, COUNT(*) AS num_pokemon
FROM pokemon_info
JOIN pokemon ON pokemon_info.pokemon = pokemon.name
WHERE (pokemon.formats = 'NFE' OR pokemon.formats = 'LC')
  AND (egg_group_1 = 'monster' OR egg_group_2 = 'monster')
GROUP BY gen;



8 records


gen
num_pokemon




1
14

8


4

2


6
4


7
1


8
2





Combines a join, multiple tier filters, and a grouped count in one query.


Quick stats
A few smaller lookups against the same database.
How many moves have non-neutral priority?


Code
-- number of moves with priority other than 0 (Martin)
SELECT COUNT(*) AS num_rows
FROM moves
WHERE priority <> 0;



1 records


num_rows




44





The ten lightest Pokémon:


Code
-- top 10 lightest pokemon (Martin)
SELECT pokemon, weight
FROM pokemon_info
ORDER BY weight
LIMIT 10;

Code
-- number of pokemon per primary type, highest to lowest (Martin)
SELECT type_1, COUNT(*) AS type_count
FROM pokemon_info
GROUP BY type_1
ORDER BY type_count DESC;

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text:
Visualizations

Average base-stat total by generation
Which generation introduced the strongest Pokémon on average? We average the six base stats across every Pokémon in each generation.


SQL: average stat total per generation
SELECT generation_id,
       AVG(hp + attack + defense + special_attack + special_defense + speed) AS total
FROM pokemon_info
WHERE generation_id IS NOT NULL   -- exclude regional forms with no listed generation
GROUP BY generation_id;



8 records


generation_id
total




1
407.6424


2
407.1800


3
403.7259


4
445.7570


5
425.7564


6
429.5833


7
449.4091


8
440.5841







Plot: average stat total per generation
generation_plot <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con_pokemon,
  "SELECT generation_id,
          AVG(hp + attack + defense + special_attack + special_defense + speed) AS total
   FROM pokemon_info
   WHERE generation_id IS NOT NULL
   GROUP BY generation_id;"
)

ggplot(generation_plot, aes(x = generation_id, y = total)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = total)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(total)), nudge_y = 10) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red") +
  labs(x = "Generation", y = "Average Stat Total",
       title = "Average Base Stats by Generation") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









Average base-stat totals stay fairly close across generations, with later generations trending slightly higher.


Move power distribution by type
How hard does each move type hit? We look at the spread of base power per type, excluding status moves (which have 0 power).


SQL: damaging-move power by type
SELECT type, power
FROM moves
WHERE power > 1   -- drop status / non-damaging moves
ORDER BY power DESC;



Displaying records 1 - 10


type
power




Normal
250


Normal
200


Fire
180


Psychic
160


Fire
160


Grass
160


Water
160


Dragon
160


Water
150


Grass
150







Plot: move power distribution by type
type_plot <- DBI::dbGetQuery(con_pokemon,"SELECT type, power FROM moves WHERE power > 1")

ggplot(type_plot, aes(x = reorder(type, power, FUN = median), y = power, fill = type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45), legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Move Power by Type",
       x = "Move Type", y = "Base Power")









Ordered by median power: even though Normal has the single highest-powered move, its median is near the bottom — while Fire is consistently high, with the top median.

title: SQL Project: Competitive Pokémon Database
text:
Sources & tools

Competitive Pokémon tier dataset (pokemon_df, moves_df) — Kaggle
Pokémon info dataset (pokemon_info_df) — TidyTuesday, 2025-04-01
AI tools (LLM) were used as an aid for dataset editing during development.
